did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep convolutional neural network (ResNet50) with transfer learning and the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) for multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset to aid in melanoma detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work evaluating the effect of SMOTE on a ResNet50 model using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific sections for 'Proposed Method', 'Training stage', 'Results and Discussion' with quantitative metrics (e.g., 94.57% accuracy), and describes a novel application of data balancing techniques, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Evaluation of the Effect of Synthetic Oversampling on Deep Neural Networks for Skin Lesion Analysis
journal: 2025 International Conference on Recent Advances in Mathematics and Informatics (ICRAMI)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the impact of the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) on the performance of a pre-trained ResNet50 deep convolutional neural network for multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The research addresses the challenge of class imbalance in medical imaging, where benign cases often outnumber malignant ones, by applying SMOTE to synthetically balance the training data. The model was trained and tested on a stratified split of the dataset, with performance assessed using accuracy, sensitivity, and confusion matrices. Results showed that the baseline model without SMOTE achieved only approximately 70% accuracy with high false negative rates for minority classes. In contrast, integrating SMOTE significantly improved the model's robustness and generalization, yielding an overall test accuracy of 94.57%. The findings demonstrate that synthetic oversampling effectively mitigates class imbalance bias and enhances the reliability of AI-assisted melanoma detection systems.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Dermatofibroma, Actinic Keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular skin lesions) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification using a ResNet50 model with a dense final layer of seven output neurons activated via a softmax function. The task is explicitly framed as a multi-class classification problem. Additionally, a binary decision rule for melanoma detection is derived from the probabilistic output of the MEL class compared against other categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark collection of 10,015 high-quality, dermatologist-annotated dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions, collected from diverse geographic regions (Europe, Australia, North America) and made available through the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) and Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet50 model (both baseline and SMOTE-augmented versions) after stratified splitting.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter validation and model evaluation during the training phase.
use.testing: Used for independent testing and final performance assessment (accuracy, sensitivity, confusion matrix) of the model.
use.other: Served as the primary benchmark for evaluating the effect of synthetic oversampling (SMOTE) on deep neural networks for skin lesion analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the HAM10000 dataset into training, validation, and testing subsets to ensure fair and unbiased evaluation and maintain class distribution.
To assess overall predictive precision and compare model performance before and after SMOTE integration.
To evaluate the model's ability to correctly identify minority classes (particularly melanoma) and reduce false negative rates.
To analyze classification performance across all seven lesion categories and identify specific misclassifications.
To measure the proportion of positive identifications that were actually correct, alongside loss curves to evaluate convergence.
To visualize and compare the stability and convergence behavior of the model during training with and without SMOTE.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the baseline model and the SMOTE-augmented model were made using descriptive metrics such as accuracy (70% vs 94.57%), sensitivity, and confusion matrices, without statistical significance testing.

Experiment 1 (Baseline without SMOTE).description: Model trained on raw, imbalanced dataset

ResNet50.notes: Exhibited irregular and unstable convergence patterns. Showed notable weaknesses in class differentiation, particularly for minority classes. High false negative rate observed. Model disproportionately favored majority classes, most prominently Melanocytic nevi (NV).

Experiment 2 (With SMOTE Integration).description: Model trained on dataset balanced using Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE)

ResNet50.notes: Demonstrated markedly improved stability and smoother convergence. Pronounced decrease in false negatives for underrepresented categories (Vascular skin lesions (VASC) and Dermatofibroma (DF)). Significant gains in sensitivity and overall predictive precision. Combined L1-L2 penalty (L1=10^-2, L2=10^-3) and dropout regularization were used to counter potential overfitting from synthetic instances.

HAM10000.notes: The integration of SMOTE significantly improved the model's robustness and generalization. The accuracy increased from 70% to 94.57%. The study confirms that class imbalance is a major limiting factor in automatic melanoma detection, leading to loss of sensitivity and increased false negative rates, which SMOTE effectively mitigated.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on an extensive image dataset (ImageNet implied by context of standard ResNet50 usage, but text states 'extensive image dataset [10]')

architecture_details.backbone: ResNet50 deep convolutional neural network with 50 convolutional layers organized into residual blocks with identity skip connections

training_details.data_split: Stratified split: 70% training (33,796 images), 10% validation (3,752 images), 20% testing (9,387 images)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 28 × 28 pixels with three color channels
Data balancing using Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) with k=5 nearest neighbors to match minority class sample counts to the majority class
Normalization of pixel intensity scaling to ensure consistent scaling across images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to image data resized to 28x28 pixels with three color channels. Configured with k=5 nearest neighbors. Oversampling ratio adjusted so that the number of samples in each minority class matched that of the majority class. Implemented using the imblearn library in Python. Used to synthetically generate new instances for underrepresented classes to mitigate class imbalance.
Applied as a stochastic regularization strategy during the learning phase to randomly suppress neuronal activations, reducing overfitting and enhancing generalization capability.

Used to constrain excessive weight adaptation and promote model generalization, specifically to counter potential overfitting from synthetic instances generated by SMOTE. Settings: L1 = 10^-2, L2 = 10^-3.

conclusions: Class imbalance is a major limiting factor in the automatic detection of melanoma, leading to a loss of sensitivity and an increased false negative rate.
Incorporating the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) significantly improved the performance of the ResNet50 model, increasing overall test accuracy from 70% to 94.57%.

SMOTE integration resulted in better detection of minority classes and a pronounced decrease in false negatives for underrepresented categories, specifically vascular lesions (VASC) and dermatofibroma (DF).
The baseline model trained on the imbalanced dataset exhibited irregular and unstable convergence patterns and disproportionately favored majority classes, particularly melanocytic nevi (NV).
Post-SMOTE training demonstrated markedly improved stability and smoother convergence, indicating a more balanced learning dynamic across all lesion categories.
Synthetic oversampling is validated as an effective rebalancing strategy for highly imbalanced medical datasets, promoting greater algorithmic fairness by limiting bias towards majority classes.

To counter the potential risk of overfitting from synthetic instances, the model utilized dropout regularization and a combined L1-L2 penalty (L1 = 10^-2, L2 = 10^-3) to constrain excessive weight adaptation and promote generalization.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which, while diverse, may not fully represent global skin tone variations or other geographic regions, limiting external validation generalizability.
SMOTE was applied to 28x28 pixel images; applying SMOTE in pixel space rather than feature space may introduce unrealistic synthetic artifacts compared to feature-space SMOTE.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance improvement, relying instead on descriptive metrics (accuracy increase from 70% to 94.57%).
There is a potential risk of data leakage or overfitting due to the use of SMOTE on the training set, although the authors mitigated this with dropout and L1-L2 regularization.
The model was trained and tested in a Kaggle computational environment, which may differ from clinical deployment infrastructure, raising concerns about real-world deployment feasibility.
The binary melanoma detection rule is derived post-hoc from the multi-class softmax output, which may not be optimal compared to a dedicated binary classification architecture.
The study focuses on a single architecture (ResNet50); results may not generalize to other deep learning models or transfer learning approaches.

The dataset contains images from diverse geographic regions (Europe, Australia, North America), but the specific demographic breakdown (age, gender, skin type) is not detailed, potentially masking population biases.
